title: Age and family life
summary: 9699 — childhood, youth, ageing, and intergenerational relationships in family contexts.
learningObjectives: Explain how childhood has changed historically.
Analyse parent-child relationships across life stages.
Evaluate care of elderly relatives and dependency.

simpleExplanation.title: Different ages, different family roles
simpleExplanation.summary: Childhood is socially constructed; youth and old age bring distinct family obligations, from care to conflict over resources.
steps: Beanpole families — fewer siblings, longer lives.

flashcards.back: Vertical extension — more generations alive, fewer children per generation.
Adults caring for children and ageing parents simultaneously.
More elderly dependants; state vs family care debates.
Family internalises shared values — personality stabilisation.

sections.content: **Age and family** (4.2) completes Paper 2 Topic 4. Link to **demographic change** (ageing population, beanpole families).

sections.question: Assess the view that childhood is a recent invention rather than a natural stage of life. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Aries argument:** Pre-industrial children worked alongside adults; **no separate childhood culture**; high infant mortality limited emotional investment.

**Modern childhood:** Compulsory **schooling**, **child protection** law, consumer culture targeting children — childhood as **protected dependency**.

**Criticisms:** **Cross-cultural** variation today; **class** — poor children still adult responsibilities; **romanticised** view ignores historical abuse; **postmodern** — children active media users not passive.

**Evaluation:** Childhood **socially constructed** and **historically variable** — not purely 'natural' but **biological immaturity** real.

**Conclusion:** **Institutional childhood** is modern invention; age category exists but **meaning** changes.
